news_summary: NASA made a groundbreaking discovery with an unprecedented 19-day solar radio burst, offering new insights into solar activity.
news_summary_fr: La NASA a fait une découverte révolutionnaire grâce à une émission radio solaire d'une durée sans précédent de 19 jours, qui apporte un nouvel éclairage sur l'activité solaire.
news_summary_es: La NASA ha realizado un descubrimiento revolucionario gracias a una ráfaga de radio solar sin precedentes que duró 19 días, lo que ha aportado nuevos conocimientos sobre la actividad solar.

articles.title: NASA stunned as strange solar radio burst lasts 19 days
articles.summary: NASA scientists were stunned when a strange radio signal from the Sun refused to fade away. Instead of lasting a few hours or days like normal solar radio bursts, this one persisted for an astonishing 19 days — shattering the previous record. Using a fleet of spacecraft spread across the solar system, researchers tracked the mysterious signal to a massive magnetic structure on the Sun called a helmet streamer.
articles.content:
When NASA scientists first picked up a radio burst coming from the Sun in August 2025, the signal initially appeared routine. Solar radio bursts are fairly common and usually fade within hours or, in some cases, a few days.

This event turned out to be anything but ordinary.

Instead of disappearing, the radio burst continued for an astonishing 19 days, setting a new record for this type of solar activity. The previous longest known event lasted only five days, making the newly observed burst far more persistent than scientists expected.

Strange Solar Radio Signal Breaks Previous Records

The event belonged to a category known as Type IV radio bursts. These bursts are produced by groups of energetic electrons trapped within the Sun's powerful magnetic fields. Although the radio waves themselves do not pose a danger to Earth, the same magnetic conditions can also trigger solar eruptions capable of sending harmful particles into space.

Those particles can interfere with satellites, spacecraft, and other space based technologies near Earth, making these solar events important for scientists who study space weather.

To better understand the unusual burst, researchers combined observations from several spacecraft positioned throughout the inner solar system. The missions included NASA's STEREO (Solar Terrestrial Relations Observatory), Parker Solar Probe, and Wind spacecraft, along with ESA (the European Space Agency) and NASA's Solar Orbiter mission.

Multiple Spacecraft Followed the Solar Burst

Because the Sun rotates, different spacecraft were able to observe the radio burst as it moved into their field of view over the course of the 19 day event. Each mission captured several days of data, allowing scientists to piece together a more complete picture of the prolonged solar activity.

Using information from the STEREO mission, the research team also created a new technique to identify the source of the burst. Their analysis traced the signal back to a massive magnetic structure in the Sun's atmosphere known as a helmet streamer.

Scientists believe the long lasting burst may have been sustained by three coronal mass ejections that erupted from the same region of the Sun. These enormous explosions release clouds of charged particles and magnetic energy into space.

Improving Space Weather Forecasting

The findings were published in the journal Astrophysical Journal Letters and could help researchers better recognize long duration solar radio bursts in the future.

By improving scientists' understanding of these unusual solar events, the research may also strengthen space weather forecasting, helping protect satellites and spacecraft from dangerous solar activity.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific discovery about an unprecedented 19-day solar radio burst, tracked by multiple spacecraft, which advances understanding of solar activity and space weather. This has broad implications for protecting satellites and space technology, representing a meaningful breakthrough in space science with tangible benefits for society.
articles.personality_title: NASA tracks record-breaking 19-day solar radio burst
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Solar radio bursts are common signals from the Sun caused by energetic electrons trapped in its magnetic fields. Usually, these bursts last only a few hours or days and help scientists study solar activity.

**What happened** – In August 2025, NASA detected a solar radio burst that lasted an incredible 19 days, far longer than the previous record of five days. Using several spacecraft—including NASA's STEREO, Parker Solar Probe, Wind, and ESA’s Solar Orbiter—scientists followed the burst as it moved with the Sun’s rotation. They traced the signal to a large magnetic structure on the Sun called a helmet streamer.

**Impact** – This discovery is important because it shows solar activity can last much longer than thought. Understanding these long bursts helps scientists predict space weather better. Space weather affects satellites and spacecraft, so better forecasts can protect important technology that we rely on for communication, navigation, and more.

**What's next step** – Researchers will use this new knowledge and the tracking method developed to identify and study other long-lasting solar radio bursts. This will improve space weather forecasting and help prepare for solar events that might disrupt technology in space.

**One-sentence takeaway** – NASA’s observation of a record 19-day solar radio burst improves how we track and understand solar activity, helping protect satellites and space technology from solar storms.
articles.personality_title_fr: La NASA observe une émission radio solaire record de 19 jours
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les émissions radio solaires sont des signaux courants venant du Soleil, causés par des électrons énergétiques piégés dans ses champs magnétiques. Habituellement, ces émissions durent quelques heures ou jours et aident les scientifiques à étudier l’activité solaire.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – En août 2025, la NASA a détecté une émission radio solaire qui a duré 19 jours, bien plus longtemps que le précédent record de cinq jours. Grâce à plusieurs engins spatiaux, dont STEREO, Parker Solar Probe, Wind et Solar Orbiter de l’ESA, les chercheurs ont suivi cette émission pendant la rotation du Soleil. Ils ont localisé le signal dans une grande structure magnétique appelée casque de streamer.

**Impact** – Cette découverte est importante car elle montre que l’activité solaire peut durer beaucoup plus longtemps qu’on ne le pensait. Mieux comprendre ces émissions longues aide à prévoir le climat spatial, ce qui est essentiel pour protéger les satellites et les engins spatiaux.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs utiliseront cette nouvelle méthode pour identifier et étudier d’autres émissions radio solaires de longue durée. Cela améliorera les prévisions du climat spatial et préparera mieux aux tempêtes solaires qui pourraient perturber la technologie spatiale.

**Résumé en une phrase** – L’observation par la NASA d’une émission radio solaire record de 19 jours améliore la surveillance de l’activité solaire et aide à protéger les satellites et la technologie spatiale des tempêtes solaires.
articles.personality_title_es: La NASA detecta una emisión de radio solar récord de 19 días
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Las emisiones de radio solares son señales comunes del Sol causadas por electrones energéticos atrapados en sus campos magnéticos. Por lo general, estas emisiones duran solo unas horas o días y ayudan a los científicos a estudiar la actividad solar.

**Qué pasó** – En agosto de 2025, la NASA detectó una emisión de radio solar que duró 19 días, mucho más que el récord anterior de cinco días. Usando varias naves espaciales, incluyendo STEREO, Parker Solar Probe, Wind y Solar Orbiter de la ESA, los científicos siguieron la emisión mientras el Sol giraba. Localizaron la señal en una gran estructura magnética llamada casco streamer.

**Impacto** – Este hallazgo es importante porque muestra que la actividad solar puede durar mucho más tiempo de lo que se pensaba. Entender estas emisiones largas ayuda a mejorar las predicciones del clima espacial, lo que protege satélites y naves espaciales.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores usarán este nuevo método para identificar y estudiar otras emisiones de radio solares de larga duración. Esto mejorará las predicciones del clima espacial y ayudará a prepararse para tormentas solares que podrían afectar la tecnología en el espacio.

**Conclusión en una frase** – La observación de la NASA de una emisión de radio solar récord de 19 días mejora el seguimiento y comprensión de la actividad solar, ayudando a proteger satélites y tecnología espacial de las tormentas solares.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a radiant, swirling sun with glowing, intricate magnetic loops forming a large helmet streamer structure on its surface, surrounded by softly glowing orbs symbolizing spacecraft gently orbiting and observing the prolonged, vibrant solar radio waves streaming outward in gentle, flowing arcs over 19 days.
